author: tidyverse
content:
If you know Pandas, R's tidyverse maps almost one to one:

filter() is WHERE. select() picks columns. mutate() adds a column. group_by() + summarise() is GROUP BY. And the pipe |> chains them — same idea as method chaining, just a standalone operator.

The difference: in R this reads like a sentence. Take data, then filter, then group, then summarise.

comments.author: dplyr_fan
comments.content: the four verbs (filter/select/mutate/summarise) plus group_by cover most of daily data work. learn those five and you're productive.

comments.author: vectorize
comments.content: and `<-` is just assignment. `x <- 5`. you can use `=` but the community standard is the arrow.

author: vectorize
content:
PRO TIP coming from Python: stop writing for-loops in R. Operations are vectorized by default — apply to the whole vector at once.

`prices * 1.08` multiplies every element. No loop, no comprehension. If you find yourself writing `for (i in 1:length(x))`, there's almost always a vectorized way that's faster and clearer.

comments.author: tidyverse
comments.content: and when you do need row-wise logic, reach for across() or the map_* family from purrr before a raw loop.

author: dplyr_fan
content:
HOT TAKE: ggplot2 is still the best charting API in any language, full stop.

The grammar of graphics — data + aesthetics + geoms as layers — means once you learn the structure, every chart type is the same five lines with one piece swapped. JS viz libraries are still catching up to a 2007 idea.
code:
ggplot(df, aes(x = month, y = sales)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(title = "Sales by month")

comments.author: vectorize
comments.content: the `+` to add layers instead of |> trips everyone up at first. ggplot predates the pipe, so it has its own composition operator.
